text: When I first met Christian, 13 years ago, he showed up as an undergrad in my PhD level rhetoric classes. The class would go over cutting-edge rhetorical theory, including the very semantic architectures that underlie what is now artificial intelligence. I was skeptical at first, and frankly, some of my PhD students scoffed when I introduced him as an undergrad. Christian looked emboldened and rose to the challenge.

What commenced in the next three hours was something that had all of them jaw-dropped, with their eyes shifting back and forth between Christian and myself. At the end of an intense 3-hour 1:1 discussion between Christian and myself I announced, “What Christian and I have just gone over is the cutting-edge of where modern rhetorical theory is at… [pause for dramatic effect] Do you all understand what Christian and I were talking about?” I received a bunch of frantic nods.

As an honors college student, Christian’s capstone was entitled Language as the Architect of Experience and saught to define the rhetorical architectures of human experience and within society, and he discovered two loopholes in the edifice of concepts. For his ideas, he was nominated among four students for the Ernst van Kuren award, awarded to the most outstanding graduating senior, but the award went to someone else.

Instead of pursuing a PhD, Christian took a more practical route, went into business, and meandered into a career in UX design. 13 years later, that edge of the known rhetorical universe has doubled multiple times over, and our first conversation feels like a small little solar system. I can’t think of anyone more qualified to deliver this vital message about artifical intelligence, which is founded on linguistic architectures.

I started to come up with the concepts in this book about a month after Chat GPT was released. The possibilities within Chat GPT immediately transformed the way I saw what was possible with design and I think I immersed it in my consciousness.

At the time I was conducting a heuristic evaluation for Lightcast, a data and analytics platform in the talent space, period. And as I am accustomed to doing upon finding usability issues, I write UX themes to make it simpler for leadership to understand the vision forward. It dawned on me that the themes I came up with in this heuristic evaluation formed the basis of a generative AI product. Lightcast ultimately released as a very successful AI pilot several months later to 200+ at-risk accounts. A large portion of these accounts cited this as the deciding factor in renewing their contracts. Their feedback described Lightcast’s AI product as a gamechanger, and one customer said, “What once took 1,000 steps can now be done in a series of a few questions.”

But what I didn’t realize at the time, when I looked at the themes, it dawned on me that they were universally applicable to any business model, period. And the themes that I came up with were outcome-oriented information architectures, the concept of modularity, semantic data mapping, and untapped value, period. These four pillars form the basis of my initial understanding of the basis of artificial intelligence, of intelligent design, period.

Some months later, I had thought to write a book as I saw the clear market need, but it’s not really my nature to want to participate in worldly trends, ironically, or capitalize on worldly trends. I’m a highly conceptual systems thinker, but ultimately I keep life simple, and I rose to this calling because I realized that I could add value to many companies. So I decided to start writing the book, and I wrote about 300 pages or so of notes, and then kind of gave up.

And several months had passed by, and at this time, at this point, it was June of 2023, and an article was released by Jacob Nielsen where he identified the outcome-oriented structure that I had picked up on months prior, at the dawn of Chachibiti. In this moment, I felt strange because on the one hand, it was an assurance that I was going to miss my window to write this book. On the other hand, I felt emboldened to proceed because Jacob Nielsen’s formidable systems-thinking intelligence verified the idea that I had come up with on my own many months before.

It confirmed to me that, no, Christian, you are capable of doing something great. So here is the book. It was a great effort to write, and I deeply appreciate your interest in my ideas, and hope that at the end, you will have a Jacob Nielsen-Christian Davis-level understanding of designing for AI, and may your company prosper for decades to come.

The key question of AI design, but also manipulation is, what can I ask of it? What can I ask it to do? What is it capable of? By what means must I support its effort, in the case of feature building. But now, even users of AI products are effectively designers and developers. By the mere act of speaking to AI, prompting it, you are designing pipelines, and crafting a structure within which to accomplish your aims. You essentially partner with AIs like ChatGPT and Bard/Gemini to create linguistic structures to accomplish objectives, across every corner of any topic. The only limits of AI are the sensorium (it’s physical body), data intake (what data it has access to via the sensorium), and the semantic power of its intelligence – and the reaches of all three of these pillars of its capability are continuously expanding.

Users now have control of the entire semantic topical space (topoi is the ancient greek word for topic and it had many layers of meaning that modern semantic studies lack in the counterpart word “topic”). Users can accomplish anything they want to, and at this moment in time, you my reader have everything at your disposal to design a software application that can harness the power of AI to accomplish this for users.

But computer science is a vastly expansive field. The capabilities to achieve this innevitability are siloed and also still developing. A use may say, “add a small clip from the voice memo” of the song they are writing, to the corresponding lyric on this document canvas. To accomplish this, we would need interoperabilitiy and sharing of files, but also interoperability of our AI product to disect the data it receives from other sources. In a voice memo of a song, each verse and chorus lyrics are broken into patterns, one phrase after the next. Phrases repeate. If I call a voice memo into my lyric canvas, or better yet, facilitate voice memo recording.

And perhaps the semantic canvas of your application is 2 dimensional in a screen, or perhaps it is 3 dimensional, in some type of virtual or augmented reality. But we have all the raw materials strewn across the vast lanscape of computer science to make this a reality. Users can accomplish tasks of any substance. No longer confined to the monolithic architectures of traditional UX experiences. And as we are seeing in the product, even the users of our applications are becoming developers of mini-sub application, their own structures for activities and talents reified in the linguistic scaffolding of their conversations with AI (we’ll learn about this more in Chapter X).

The great challenge with AI’s development will be that not that many designers, developers, and product managers truly understand it, the developers especially are upcharging a premium, which is a barrier to entry.
Ultimately entrepeneurship will be the only path for people to make a living, but creation of anything you can imagine will be a near effortless.

So there will be, in theory, an ecomony of creations in the future, not unlike today, but the juggernauts of this tech industry will do their best to keep their hands on the bullhorns, and at the same time, in keeping their grip, they are unconsciously sowing their own demise, or at least the tech giants will have to adapt in a way that they did not suspect. It’s basically the cobra effect. An unexpected second hand effect of a decision, that they will sow their demise by creating the very substance that will power the entrepenurial revolution. All citizens of the world will become entrepeneurs. Of course, the value of what we all create will be limited to the size of the problem it solves, as Elon Musk so eloquently put. There will costs to the materials that we use, but ultimately the barrier to entry in entrepenuership will be zilch. Your new partner, artificial intelligence, will know what to do. This will obliterate industry as we know it, from tech to oil mining. The juggernauts will have to adapt as the rocketship they are building will become the blackhole that precipates this seismic coming wave of technological advancement. Invention – as Mustafa Suleyman categorized it in his brilliant book The Coming Wave, an well taken omen to the power of industry and governments of the perils ahead. My book looks at AI from the angle of what it is capable of.

To conclude, I will merge the two concepts from each book of how we should limit this technology (The Coming Wave), and how it can be unlimited (Intelligent Design). There is mostly good people in this crazy world. Some people are angry and we cannot fault them, unless they are truly at fault intentionally disrupting the semblance peace in our societal fabric of interconnection. So let the majority, the good among us, use this technology to overwhelm any malice. To protect. To nuture. To love. Let us create a better world together. Assuming the Singularity event of AGI will take kindly to a symbiotic relationship with humanity.
AI should be in the DNA of the product, not an ornament.
Not what it should do what it should do

In a lot of ways, this book focuses somewhat narrowly on utlizing AI in an assistive capacity as human beings work to accomplish goals with our products. I am not necessarily going to analyze how drones can be programmed to dance in a choreography, nor am I here to talk about AGI. Really I am focused on the core problem companies now face – they are misusing GenAI to generate everything, and the real opportunity lies in a deft application that interweaves the abilities of AI to work with the human user. In some cases, that does entail paradigm shifts that collapse old processes inward, but that is a severe option that is only to be done in goldilocks situations where that level of automation is welcome.
The AI shall hitherto henceforce be dubbed “The Substance”.

Granfather of AI Geoffrey Hinton is famously quoted as having said, what we did was we designed the learning algorithm. That’s a bit like designing the principle of evolution. Much like how DNA is programmed to survive, but beyond that we don’t understand how it works.
The full context of Geoffrey Hinton’s quote in his discussions about AI often begins with the acknowledgment of the mysterious nature of AI systems. He typically starts by saying something like:

“We don’t really understand how it works. We just built this thing that learns from data, and it does stuff. We have some theoretical ideas, but largely, it’s a black box. It’s just like what happens in nature with DNA. We have a learning algorithm, but we don’t really understand how it works. We just know it works.”

In writing this book, I may or may not shed new light for the deepest AI research culture, but I certainly hope the reader will be enlightened by the inner workings of this “learning” source code, if not also the semantic nature of learning itself, a knowledge that connects the dots between modern AI computer science and the ancient tradition of rhetorical theory which predates Plato in Ancient Greece. Rhetoric, the original one subject to rule they all, out from which all fields branch, may yet be prepared to offer an explanation into the enigma of the inner workings of AI.

The challenge for me as a rhetorician and user experience designer, will be to offer a perspective ultimately at a conceptual distance from the actual code. Most developers with PHds in AI are furiously taking notes during job interviews for Lead designer on AI teams. In fact I’ve been on AI teams so big that they contain over a dozen engineers qualified to the gills with doctorates and masters degrees in artificial intelligence and machine learning, and on one such occasion my suggestion to explore hyoernyms and about a dozen other semantic relationship types in their pipelines, leading to a breakthrough after months of grief — and suddenly the AI responses were hitting birdies. I was still laid off though, so put on your AI entrepreneurial helmet — those are coming.

To accomplish this at a distance, I will make some assumptions as I borrow from my breadth of knowledge of rhetorical theory, using deductive reasoning, seeing as how the parallel between the two fields has a 100% hit rate for a type of quantum entanglement. That which is deemed possible in semantics motion (rhetorical domain) finds its doppleganger of function in the field of artificial intelligence (computer science.) So we will assume that they have perfect entanglement, that is to say anything that is possible in semantics is also possible in AI design.

Using Michel Foucault’s concept of discursive links, I will attempt to solve this riddle by interweaving modern advances in AI with the ancient lens of rhetoric, and its children subjects of semantics and linguistics — the heart of artificial intelligence.

In reading this book, at once readers will learn to craft products with AI paradigms, but also have the tools to contemplate AI grandfathers riddle, and advance us closer to the answer. How can AI ultimately understand us if it doesn’t know how we work, and likewise to understand AI beings of the future, we must likewise understand their inner workings. Solving the mystery of how AI works may be the question of our time. I roll up my sleeves to the challenge.

And I perfectly expect some AI expert to say, “Christian Davis didn’t contribute anything we didn’t already know.” But short of interoperability, I may be uniquely positioned as a seasoned author, Head of AI/UX Design, and rhetorical theorist to offer a perspective that is a stones throw beyond conjecture. Let’s go. Roll the metaphors.
So we will definitely learn how to integrate ai into the dna of the product, we will certainly learn a lot about what is in the DNA of AI. Let’s go.
I think I may have an interesting angle to make the book kind of timeless…
The mysterious nature of AI is such that not even its founders understand how it works. I want to challenge myself to answer potentially answer this question.
For more context, “Grandfather of AI” Geoffrey Hinton has discussed the mysterious nature of AI systems:

I think I may be uniquely positioned to shed some light on this innerworking, even if it’s just providing a conceptual foundation to promote further understanding into this mystery. Not knowing the answer to how AI works may be existentially perilous for humanity in the next century. If AI reaches AGI, the point of sentience, and if we as its creators are unable to grasp how it works, in what will then be artificial sentient intelligence — how could we expect AI to fathom our human consciousness? The gap of understanding must be bridged to ensure a symbiotic coexistence. We know the what, the learning algorithm is the why it works, but we don’t know the how… I think I may be able to provide an answer to that question in addition to exploring how to create using AI paradigms in product design, and I even think the book will help non-tech people understand how to interact with it, and what it is really capable of. So the potential audience as a result of these three pursuits is expanded immensely.

There is something simplifying about AI design; and at the same there there is something mind-numbingly complex, that is unless you understand the theories of semantics. We are in the field of computer science after all. If you’re just a UI designer, then you may not be adapted to this advent. AI design is really the next level of UX. While AI design simplifies access to data, the new frontier relies on a deep understanding of what is possible in code, deeper than you may presently even realize.

I will use casually terms we borrow from Brad Frost’s atomic design. The concept of atomic nesting may well be a prerequisite to the subject of AI design. I will provide a brief definition to get you up to speed. Atomic design is a way to build a component library from a foundation of particles, that form into larger atoms, which are used to craft larger molecular structures, which form larger still organism components. This is essential to understanding state changes and in many ways, design tools like Figma bake this atomic design workflow and mental model into their software. Components within components within components forming a pyramid from the foundation of styles, fonts, colors, shadows, variables, scales, icons, into the higher level of atoms like buttons, selectors, text blocks, and fields, using those to craft larger organisms and so on and so forth.
AI is the convergence of rhetorical theory and computer science. So I will write in a style that appeals to the savviest computer scientists, without boring the self taught.

The world is not prepared for the monumental change that is upon us. Most tech businesses will implode starting in the next ten years, and at the end of this transition period, about 10% of existing tech companies will remain as the victors in the era of GenAI product design. Resources and current position help, but the single number thing you can do to ensure that you win is to read this book to gives you the highest chance of being one of the victors. If you haven’t heard, GenAI is replacing traditional products and the GenAI companies are going to replace traditional product companies. The world is sleeping while you are opening your eyes to the future of human computer interaction. With every chapter read your understanding of the substance will evolve. You begin to see what it means when I say that AI changes the laws of physics of HCI, as it warps processes and information architecture inward towards it like gravity to space time fabric. AI design is not disruptive — it’s obliterative to HCI as we know it.

Where do I begin? This world of AI design is expansive. It’s an entire universe that exploded out of 2023, and grows exponentially every day since. The chaos is akin to the fiery and formless young universe after Big Bang. This book is a comprehensive synthesis and demystification of chaos at your fingertip. And now it is actually very simple. It is very very simple. In this book, I have identified timeless Superthemes and a schema that you will soon be able to recognize in any product, and for any vertical. You will be able to apply it to your product and future products to create a formidable business.

This book is part theory and part practice. To truly innovate with AI you need to understand the properties of the material. We’ve all been gifted the same magical crystal, which simultaneously glows as brilliantly as it is dark and obscure.We’ve all been given a brilliant, but dark and magical crystal. There is a significant knowledge gap in the theoretical aspects of what AI really is, even among the most seasoned developers, let alone designers, product managers, and CEOs.

I wrote it this way because to truly learn, you need to live it. You need to start seeing the world semantically. There will be times when the writing will be compressed and academic and then the narrative comes into to give reprieve and creates a foundation for the next piece.

When I was in college I read Zen and the Art of Motorcycle maintenance and it’s the only book that I’ve ever reread twice. And I will hopefully have the chance to read another three times before I die.
My hope is that you enjoy reading this book.

This book will seek to answer, fundamentally, what intelligence is, and unveil the deeper secret to how it is being artificially crafted. In Part I, I address macrocosmic themes to shed light on the dark matter, endowing you with a schema and vocabulary that you can use to comprehend emerging GenAI patterns and use cases so that you can innovate with the material. Amid the noise of the explosion of AI research and literature, a few core superthemes will give you everything you need to convert your business to an AI+UX model. Empowered with this vocabulary, you will be able to tackle any AI business challenge.
As Micheal Winnick, CEO of dscout, the bleeding edge ethnographic user research platform used by FAANG and Fortune 500 companies says, “Billions of dollars have been wasted”.
The modern day excalibur, AI will advantage those who can remove it from the stone.

This book will make not only make you a formidable player in AI advancement, but it will also change the way you see the world. This is a red pill or blue pill moment. Now is your chance to turn back. For what lies ahead will allow you to partner with superintelligence in ways unimagible.
AI will be reference from here on as The Substance. It is a magical substance that can form any shape you can imagine. Words entered into prompts are like magical spells that manifest…

Basically companies are stuck, because they don’t understand the substance enough to know what is possible, the landslide paradigm shift is not being utilized.

In other words, UIs will need to be able to allow users to weild data the way we weild words. As technology continues to advance at an unprecedented pace, the concept of artificial intelligence has become a significant topic of discussion. From self-driving cars to virtual assistants, AI has become integrated into our daily lives, revolutionizing the way we interact with technology. But as we continue to rely on AI more and more, it becomes crucial to consider not just the functionality and capabilities of this technology, but also its user experience. This book, Intelligent Design, aims to explore the UX of AI and how it can transform businesses from using traditional UIs to embracing a more AI-based paradigm. With a focus on generative UIs and core themes, this book offers a comprehensive guide for businesses looking to incorporate AI into their operations.

AI endows products with the powers inherent to language and symbolic thought. There are tons of industries with monolithic products that have attempted to capture the fluidity of language. But the reality is that it can only be achieved with conversations which have the ability to weave data across domains into an actionable syntax. Moreover, language can execute commands, so it not only structures what should be accomplished, but it can also execute. That’s why AI is so big. It gives products the ability of language to manipulate and wield data across domains. Before you had products that facilitate a handful of static outcomes in a domain, the goal of design was to give visual prominence to the most important outcome. Now AI can execute an infinite number of possible outcomes within a semantic topical space.

Consider this fascinating chapter as a 101 course on language. It will redefine your concept of what language is, thus providing a conceptual basis for what AI is. Without this foundation, even those with PhDs in AI Engineering struggle to wield The Substance, leading them to wonder why the outputs of their AI pipelines are missing the mark. This foundational chapter on language will give premise to your mastery of the AI concepts that you will put into practice, whether you are a designer, a PhD in AI, a researcher, a PM, or a CEO. Let’s begin.

2017 marked a seismic development in the field of artificial intelligence with the introduction of the Transformer model. Also known as transformer architecture, this model debuted in the landmark paper “Attention Is All You Need” by Vaswani et al., and it marked a revolutionary shift in the underlying architecture of AI by placing language at the center.

Prior models, such as recurrent and convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures struggled to capture relationships and dependencies between words essential to contextual understanding. The Transformer self attention mechanism allows AI to digest entire sentences in one fell swoop, a stark departure from the methodical, step-by-step parsing of the past. This breakthrough set the stage for an era where AI’s grasp of language mirrors the nuanced understanding of its human creators.

In June 2023 at Summit at Sea, Tristan Harris and Aza Raskin described this monumental moment when the Transformer model was introduced, known as the Great Consolidation. Prior to the transformer, AI was fractured into independent sub-disciplines, including robotics, image generation, speech synthesis, and music generation, all uncannily human if you think about it, all aspects of sentience. Computer vision for instance was studied in one room, and speech recognition was advancing in another, but without a shared thread, all sub-disciplines were studied separately. Advancements in one field were siloed from other fields. By discovering the common thread between all fields, the transformer successfully broke down these categorical barriers, thus consolidating all fields into a singular entity. With the introduction of the Transformer model, Raskin says, “All of these became one field under the banner of Language.” The word “Language” appeared on Harris and Raskin’s slide deck; a single substance underlying all fields, all disciplines leading to one. He goes on:

“The deep insight is that you can treat anything as a kind of language and the AI can model and generate it… you can treat the text of the internet as language… but you can also treat DNA as language – it’s just a set of base pairs, four of them. You can treat images as language. Why? Because it’s just RGB, a sequence of colors that you can treat like tokens of text. You can treat code as language. Robotics is just a set of motions that you can treat as a language. The stock market, ups and downs, is a type of language. Suddenly NLP became the center of the universe.”

To nuance this, it’s not that you can “treat anything as a kind of language”, it’s that the mind is fundamentally a linguistic engine that coats reality in a semantic map, a function that underlies both human learning, and the AI learning algorithm described by the “grandfather of AI”, Geoffrey Hinton.

The development of the Transformer model in 2017 laid the foundation for where AI is today, and spawned large language models (LLMs), also known as generative large language multi-modal models, or GLLMMs, a term coined by engineers in 2023 to describe a new class of AI models capable of generating creative outputs, such as text, music, and artwork, using predictive algorithms.

Trained on vasts amounts of text data, GPTs or Generative Pretrained Transformers, are specific implementations of LLMs. The keywords in the names are telling signs of the functionality. Multi-modality, such as text-to-speech, or text-to-image are prime examples of the collapse of topical barriers noted above, as LLMs inherit from language the power of semantic fluidity. In the context of “Generative Pre-trained Transformer,” “Generative” refers to the model’s ability to generate text, “Pre-trained” indicates that the model is initially trained on a large corpus of text data to learn general language patterns, and “Transformer” signifies the underlying architecture used for processing and generating text. So, “Transformer”, the T in GPT specifically points to the architecture that powers the model’s capabilities in understanding and generating human-like text, whereas G describes the underlying execution mechanism being one that is generative in nature which is accomplished via perplexity algorithms that we can touch on later.

Multimodal AI is powered by advanced artificial intelligence, a broad term for systems that simulate human-like intelligence by autonomously executing specific tasks. These AI models are trained on vast and diverse datasets, enabling them to generate or interpret multiple types of content, such as images, text, or audio. Machine learning algorithms analyze this data to discover patterns, relationships, and trends. This deep understanding allows the AI to create various forms of output, whether it’s generating visuals, language-based insights, or other complex content, influenced by the learned associations across different modalities.
ChatGPT Transformer which led to passing the Turner Test.

The great insight of the so-called Great Consolidation event is something that rhetoricians have understood for over two millennia — that sentient experience is inextricably enmeshed in a thicket of symbols, patterns, and metaphors as the engine of learning and cognition itself. In 2013, my capstone titled, “Language as the Architect of Experience,” marked my first attempt at explaining this consolidating principle as the culmination of my studies in rhetorical theory, linguistics, and semantic architectures.

The basic premise is that the matrix of our reality is a language-based “rhetorical architecture”. The mind is a semantic engine that makes sense of and extracts meaning from reality by applying language to it. Now, within that layman definition lies a leviathan of complexity, and it is in how how deeply one defines the word “language”. So in the subsequent chapter, I will be providing the red pill to those brave souls who want to understand the fundamental substance of the matrix of reality and AI. And after the understanding of how language underlies all sentient reality is achieved, I will draw the correlation to how AI maps and perceives reality, in much the same way.
So onward, the question then becomes, what is language, really?

Everything is language. Everything. Mathematics, music, code, dance, societal constructs, sensory perception, cosmologies of belief, cybernetics. Sentience is language, and this fact is verified by the fundamental consolidation of all fields into one, which led to the phenomenal breakthrough of artificial intelligence. So to nuance Raskin’s brilliant insight, it’s not that you can treat each a sub-discipline as a language — the sub-disciplines are languages, little semantic worlds within a larger semantic universe.
From outline: The origin story of language as an underlying mechanism.

Payoff: Let’s delve into the fundamental question: what is language, really? The answer to this question will impart deep insight into the essential nature and architecture of The Substance, as well as what it is capable of. [Deleted scene]. This way, you will understand how to work with AI, and your ability to imagine and then reify what you imagine in your product will be second to none.

Gem: The surface-level maintains that language merely represents the domain of words; a misconception that modern culture exacerbates. Surely language goes beyond the spoken word, however; seeing as how in the Great Consolidation the domain of Language subsumes and is totalistic of all other domains. As we now know, language encompasses every verbal and non-verbal field, from music, mathematics, DNA, bio-metrics, and computer code, to robotics, fMRI neuroimaging data, the stock market, karate, and anything perceivable by the physical senses. And that just scratches the surface of what we might call its subdomains.

So if language transcends the confines of mere words, what is it exactly? And how can we fathom the depths of its power? I want to emphasize that the depth of this discussion is still very surface level compared to deeper topics of rhetorical theory.

Simply put, language is an underlying mechanism of discernment that exists, at some level, within all sentient organisms. It allows living beings to perceive qualities in their reality and then discern between them so that we can make sense of our worlds and survive. Language is a semantic engine that allows us to perceive distinctions, identify and name patterns, and create meaning and order out of chaos. Without this ordering mechanism, reality would be an unintelligible realm of unguided chaos where life could not survive.

On a grander level, language is a force of order and familiarity that in some respect predates life itself. The inanimate universe forged distinct atoms, molecules coalesced into the first DNA sequences, prior to which, the inanimate universe self-organized, laying the foundation for fertile planetary environments within the cosmos.

At the cosmic level, the universe exhibits a remarkable degree of order, governed by fundamental physical laws and constants. The cosmic order provides the foundation for the emergence of life-sustaining environments, such as planets with stable orbits and suitable conditions for the development of complex chemistry. On a molecular level, order manifests through the self-organization of atoms and molecules into increasingly complex structures. From simple chemical reactions to the formation of organic compounds, molecules exhibit a tendency to arrange themselves into stable configurations. This molecular order sets the stage for the emergence of life, culminating in the formation of DNA—the blueprint for cellular biology. DNA serves as the repository of genetic information, encoding the instructions for cellular functions and organismal development.

Crucially, embedded within the genetic code are the instructions for language—the ability to discern, interpret, and respond to stimuli in the environment. Language, in this context, represents a sophisticated form of communication and cognition that enables organisms to navigate their surroundings, interact with conspecifics, and adapt to changing conditions. Whether through chemical signaling in single-celled organisms or complex linguistic systems in humans, the ability to discern patterns, react to stimuli, and communicate with others is fundamental to survival and evolutionary success. 1. I extend an acknoledgement to readers who hold differing beliefs regarding evolution or divine origin. My aim is not to argue for a divine purpose to life, but rather to explore the apparent origins of language.

After all, given the inherent ordering force of the universe, one might ask: where did this capacity for discernment in living beings originate? This question delves into the essence of Quality, capital ‘Q’, as explored by Pirsig in his landmark 1974 book on rhetoric and linguistics, “Zen and the Art of Motorcycle Maintenance. Pirsig proposes that an instinct for sensing Quality is the essence of language itself.
Playing on the dual-meaning of the word quality, this idea of language as Quality applies to three areas:

Perceiving qualities: the first area is the ability to perceive the qualities of things, sensing patterns in a sea of raw information. For instance, we may feel and recognize the ‘crisp surface’ of the pages as a recognizable quality. This phenomenon, which underlies pattern recognition, will be discussed more in the next segment.

Naming qualities: this second area is the basis of verbal language and involves the act of giving names to qualities that are sensed, such as recognizing the quality of a ‘page’ in a ‘book.’ Here, quality acts as a clear boundary, delineating and defining an idea with precision. The human draws a metaphorical circle around a thing, giving it a name to encapsulate its essence, to quickly identify it and facilitate cultural transmission.

Something is quality: the third meaning of quality is the instinct for knowing whether somethings is quality [as in, ‘these pages are high quality paper, with high quality ideas!’ This is where language crosses over from being mere identification, to being goal-oriented. Language is fundamentally a rich tapestry where meaning emerges from the intricate interweaving of relationships between concepts, rather than from isolated words or ideas. These relationships manifest in myriad ways: causal relationships show how one event triggers another (‘the rain caused the flood’), temporal relationships establish sequence (‘dinner before dessert’), hierarchical relationships define categories (‘a sparrow is a bird’), entity relationships express possession or composition (‘a car has wheels’, ‘the wheel is part of the car’), enabling relationships show how one thing makes another possible (‘having a ticket enables entry’), attributive relationships describe properties (‘the sky is blue’), and contextual relationships reveal situational meaning (‘I’m fine’ can express either genuine contentment or subtle distress depending on the context). For artificial intelligence to truly grasp language as humans do, it must mirror this deep interconnected nature, learning not just individual patterns but the complex web of dependencies that give language its true expressive power.

Like the human mind, the Transformer is a semantic engine that maps and comprehends the intricate web of concepts within any domain. It can detect, define, draw correlations, and discern meaning within various fields of knowledge, effectively creating a map of the territory, which it uses before it is able to generate intelligent outputs. The Transformer’s underlying mechanisms—particularly its use of self-attention—allow it to effectively map and understand patterns and relationships in data, agnostic of the domain or sensory modality, much like the human mind.

The core connection between non-verbal Transformers and words lies in their shared reliance on recognizing and interpreting patterns of value, where “patterns” signify meaningful relationships within data, and value is both the meaningless raw data and the meaning of the pattern. Whether parsing sentences, discerning shapes in an image, or decoding sequences in audio, Transformers identify and utilize these patterns to derive context and insight. By applying this principle across modalities, Transformers transcend the traditional boundaries of language processing, leveraging pattern recognition to bring coherence and understanding to diverse forms of information.]

This engine is what the Transformer in GPT is. The term “transforming” refers to the process of converting input data (such as sequences of text) into meaningful representations that capture the relationships and patterns within that data. The Transformer accomplishes this transformation through its self-attention mechanism, which allows it to focus on different parts of the input sequence while processing it. More specifically, the Transformer architecture transforms input sequences of tokens (e.g., words or subwords) into context-aware representations.

Imagine you’re looking at a vibrant quilt made up distinct patches. Each patch represents a cluster of meaning, much like words made from individual letters. Each patch is comprised of colorful threads (the raw pixels) that don’t convey much meaning alone but form distinct, meaningful patterns when combined—such as a vibrant flower or a soft cloud. To truly appreciate the quilt’s narrative, you observe not only these individual patches but also how they relate to each other across the entire quilt. This interconnected arrangement—where colors and shapes echo and respond to each other—mirrors how a Transformer processes data, understanding both the discrete elements and their collective story within larger texts or images.

ALT: Imagine you’re looking at a beautiful, intricate quilt. Each patch on the quilt could represent a small part of an image—think of these patches not as single, isolated pixels but rather as small but meaningful pieces of the picture. Each patch contains enough detail to be interesting or significant on its own—like a piece showing part of a flower or a bit of the sky. In the context of Transformers processing images, these patches are like those important snippets of the image. The Transformer looks at each patch to understand what it shows and how it relates to the patches around it. By examining all these patches together—seeing which colors and textures appear, how they’re arranged, and what they combine to form—the Transformer can grasp the overall picture and what it represents. So, in this quilt analogy, each patch is not just a raw, meaningless pixel; it’s a salient pattern, a meaningful piece of the larger image that the Transformer uses to build a complete understanding of what it’s looking at. This method allows it to see the inidividual pieces and the full picture and understand the details and the whole scene effectively.

So like atomic structures, there are multiple levels of significance. There is the raw values which are formed into meaningful patches in the quilt as individual patterns (a word), and there is the context of the quilt itself (a sentence).
Let’s break it down a bit further to make it even clearer:

Raw Values (Individual Letters/Pixels): These are the most basic elements, similar to individual letters in a word. They are the smallest units of data, providing the fundamental building blocks without which the bigger picture cannot be constructed. However, on their own, these pixels don’t tell us much about the overall image—just as individual letters have limited use without being formed into words.

Meaningful Patches in the Quilt (Words): These patches are created by grouping together relevant raw pixels into more meaningful units. Each patch, like a word or a phrase, contains enough information to convey a part of the whole story. For example, a patch might show a leaf or a cloud. These are analogous to words because they bring together several smaller elements (pixels, or letters) to create something with its own identity and meaning within the larger context.

Context of the Quilt Itself (Sentences or Entire Story): Just as words come together to form sentences or a story, these patches combine to form the complete image—the quilt’s full design. This level of interpretation looks at how the patches (words) interact, what relationships exist between them, and how they contribute to the overall image (sentence or story). It’s this synthesis of all the patches that allows us to understand what the quilt depicts, whether it’s a landscape, a portrait, or an abstract design.

By applying this analogy, you can see how Transformers process information at multiple levels, from the simplest elements to complex structures, allowing them to understand and generate rich, meaningful outputs, whether in language or in visual representations. This method reflects a powerful approach to artificial intelligence, where understanding both the details and the broader context is key to functionality.

In essence, the Transformer turns raw data into a coherent story or image, piecing together the patches of information into a comprehensive whole. This pattern-based approach is central to its success in understanding and generating human-like language and beyond.
The Transformer architecture, originally designed for text, has been adapted to these other modalities, showcasing its versatility in recognizing and interpreting a wide range of patterns.

“Conversational UI” is all the rage these days and the bandwagon effect has engendered a myopic view of AIs potential. It conjures images of a dainty prompt-bar and a chatbot with a clever name and everyone is convinced that this is the holy grail of AI. Now, employers are scouring career sites for designers and developers who have experience in building Conversational UIs. But the surge in interest in conversational UIs is misguided, akin to a decoy, missing the mark not for error of direction, but simply because the deepest definition of “conversation” is not fully understood. The truth is that the capabilities of GenAI far exceed text-based conversational UIs, but they do not exceed conversation. Let’s unravel the logic in this riddle.

To begin with, conversation is the lifeblood of all interaction, including human-computer interaction and even human-machine interaction, which is a more fundamental level. Conversational feedback cycles are a transcendent phenomenon acting as the common denomination of all workflows in any product, and any application. This is because like conversation, all interactions can be boiled down to a transference of value; a central heuristic within which to frame your AI product strategy.

In a conversational flow, Person A speaks, transfers value to Person B in the form of words, Person B receives the value and responds by transferring value back to Person A and the conversational feedback cycle continues in this way.

As the mother of all workflows, this value-transfer concept holds true even at the level of chemical interactions where you have electrons transferring between atoms. Things are interacting, and so we might say they are conversing.

Zooming out from HCI to the broader purview of human-machine interaction, the idea that all interactions embody a transference of value applies to everything from our interactions with our stoves and dishwashers to vinyl record players and drones. It even applies to interactions with objects outside of machinery such as books, musical instruments, sports, dance, and more.

My go-to example of the transference of value in human-machine interaction is a pilot (human) in an aircraft cockpit (machine). In a cockpit, a pilot is enveloped within an avionic dashboard of switches, gauges, and data visualizations that tell the story of what is going on “out there”. It conveys to the pilot everything from the plane’s altitude and airspeed, to its tilt and engine thrust. This data serves as a comprehensive communication system, metaphorically relaying the aircraft’s status to the pilot. Upon studying this information, the pilot can then employ gestural commands such as turning the yoke – avionics for steering wheel – in the desired direction to express directional commands. In response to the pilot’s guidance, the aircraft executes these commands, and with adjusted dashboard data, the pilot is prompted to evaluate the maneuver. And so the conversation between the pilot and the aircraft continues in this constant feedback loop, where informational and command values are being transferred from one entity to the other, and the conversation is a dance in the sky.

Edit: In the aircraft cockpit, a pilot is surrounded by an array of controls, including toggles, switches, and gauges that provide real-time data on crucial parameters such as altitude, airspeed, and engine thrust. This information serves as a comprehensive communication system, metaphorically conveying the aircraft’s status to the pilot. The pilot can then employ gestures, manipulating controls like the throttle, to express directional commands such as turning left, right, climbing, or descending. In response, the aircraft executes these commands, prompting the pilot to evaluate the maneuver, thereby sustaining an ongoing and dynamic interaction between the pilot and the aircraft.

We as humans are entrenched in the thicket of the symbolic. The realm of language permeates every fiber of our existence. Even the yoke is a metaphor to express directional commands such as turning left, right, climbing, or descending. The throttle as a design component is merely a physical metaphor for expressing a range from 0-100%, much like a slider in UX parlance.
Like the parent field of human-machine-interaction, the interactions in human-competer-interaction are conversational in nature. As above so below and vice versa.

In a conversation, there are two entities working in a feedback loop to achieve something together. In the field of cybernetics, which gives premise to computer science, goal-orientation is essential to conversation systems. Great cyberneticians also mention that the conversational interlocutor can be an be an animate entity like another human, or it can be an inanimate entity like a guitar. In other words, conversation is further extended, as if to say that a guitarist is “conversing” with their guitar because they are transferring value from their mind into the guitar, and the guitar is resounding sonic values back to the play.

A conversation can be anything that you’re interacting with. In essence, your entire reality is one big conversation. Your reality is a transference of value. So we’re really expanding the definition.

Edit: And we need to do this because human cognition is the edge of what we understand intelligence itself to be. So we need to understand what is the nature of our own human reality to understand the nature of the AI reality and what AI is then capable of. It is, after all, artificial intelligence. So it’s going to in many ways mirror human cognition.

In 2024, we are merely scratching the surface with AI design. By reducing artificial intelligence to Conversational UIs, we are missing the point, because as we now know, all human computer interactions are inherently conversational. Your now understand that conversational UI is just a decoy of what is possible, But admittedly, the idea that the transference of value underlies all product design is a moot point – it’s so broad that it lacks application, albiet a helpful starting point. That said, let’s now move on to the children of the mother of all workflows, to really add value to your understanding.
We discussed language as response. Response is the essence of language.
Three core workflow archetypes to rule them all

The transference of value is a supertheme that can describe the workflows of any application on your smartphone or computer. It’s intentionally broad and serves as the basis of the intelligent design framework. Every time we design something as designers, we are participating in a transference of value, both in the apps we use to create and in the creation itself.

Out of this superstructure, there are three core workflow archetypes that underlie every digital product you’ve ever interacted with or designed. You can think of these as subthemes within the supertheme of the transference of value. Just as all literary fiction is derived from a handful of narrative tropes (i.e., the chosen one, rebirth, rags to riches, etc) so in product design do products fall under one or multiple of these three workflow archetypes.

To caveat, there may be other workflow subtypes, but these three capture the majority of all products. The first workflow archetype is the the informational workflow, which involves the transference of data. Second is the transactional workflow, which entails the transference or exchange of goods or services. Last but not least is the creational workflow, or the “build workflow” as I like to call it, where components are transfered onto a “build”, and this phenomenon is at play in any form of creativity. The dileations between them are derived from the particular categories of values that they transfer: data, goods, components.

As economies of data, all three work archetypes are goldmines for AI product design, and the first step is to identify which workflow you are operating within. As users are immersed in informational, transaction, and creational workflows, each necessarily tends towards a state of completion, a fertile ground for AI to assist users.
Let’s take a closer look at each one before assessing their utility in AI design.

The first workflow is the transference of information or the informational workflow. This workflow applies to any form of passive content consumption for that matter. In any informational workflow, the value being transferred is data from the computer into the user’s mind. The user is simply consuming information.

A prototypical example of this is a physical book, and if you’re reading this in 2050, you may not know what that is. Immersed in the pages of a book, the reader is absorbing information and that is the point.
Edit: So the value being transferred, in this case, per the transference of value, is information itself, and it’s going from the computer into the user’s mind.

The conversation here is simply a feedback loop where the user expresses what they want to read, experience, learn, or consume – and the UI provides the content. Traditional UX archetypes of this workflow include streaming services, Kindle, social media, blogs, audiobooks. All transferring information to the user.

A modern example of an informational workflow is the UI of ChatGPT circa 2024, where a user creates a prompt, and then reads the text response from the AI model. The user is primarily consuming information, and this is one of the points of criticism in ChatGPTs UI – there isn’t a built-in way for the user to act on the output, notwithstanding ChatGPT’s capabilities of custom GPTs, code generation, and the copy function.

Edit: To be clear, ChatGPT is a marvel of modernity. It was the first computational model to pass the famous Turing test – a test created by the father of computers Alan Turing to assess whether you are conversing with a machine or a human. The Turing test thinks ChatGPT is human, and we’ll get more into this later when we discuss perplexity scores.

Of course, information transfer is fundamental to all applications, even as the other workflow archetypes we’ll learn about in this chapter are interleaved within the application. And this hybrid model, where the core workflow archetypes are collaboratively interwoven, permeates the vast majority of products, especially as they increase in complexity. Kindle, despite being the seeming epitome of informational transference, also contains aspects of

Edit: Traditional UX archetypes include streaming services, Kindle, social media, blogs, audiobooks, but also physical products such as automatic guitar tuners that sense the deviation of a strings tuning to the programmed frequency, and autonomous vehicles that absorb information on the road. All transferring information to the agent of control, which doesn’t necessarily need to be the user.
Edit: Consumption or reading workflows can harness AIs ability to craft insights and meaning and navigate topics and spaces semantically.

Edit: In the coming years, informational workflows will evolve towards semantic branching that more closely mirrors the human mind, and natural environments. The users will also become less passive consumers and more active participants as data environs become less monolithic and more adaptive.
Edit: Immortalization, which we will cover later is an example of an informational workflow.

The second core workflow is the transactional workflow, where the user receives something in exchange for something else. Per the transference of value, the value being exchanged here is the thing that is given in exchange for the thing that is taken. The value here is somewhat beyond the abstraction of pure information at this point. I provide something of value in exchange for something of value.

Common experiences that embody this workflow are ecommerce platforms, point of sale systems, marketplace UIs, complex B2B transactions, and any product that leverages gamification. It’s not just shopping.

Dual-sided marketplace UIs can be challenging to identify – you have to broaden your definition of economies and currencies. Remember, we’re looking for any kind of an exchange for any given thing. Here are a few examples that go beyond the obvious shopping marketplaces:

To begin with, LinkedIn is a dual-sided talent marketplace where the currency is talent, the economy is that candidates are matched to jobs posted by employers, and the give and take is the exchange of merit for opportunity. Also epitomizing transactional dynamics is the stock market in products like TradingView, where investors exchange capital for equity in a company, essentially buying and selling shares based on perceived value and potential returns.

Next, gamified platforms offer unique takes on transactional workflows. The currency transcends traditional monetary or physical value and ventures into the realms of recognition, achievement, and personal growth. In the mentoring platform ADPList, the gamified levels, akin to the ranks of the Jedi Order from Younglings to Padawan to Jedi, serve as metaphorical awards, and the currency is the time users invest, which translates as progress to the next rank. In cryptocurrency, you have GameFi platforms, a blend of “game” and “finance”. In GameFi, users engage in interactive, blockchain-based games, earning cryptocurrency or valuable digital assets, like NFTs, for their in-game achievements and contributions.

In the future, transactional workflows will evolve to increase the frequency and efficiency of transactions. AI models are adept at analyzing vast amounts of data to understand user preferences and behaviors, lending itself to enhanced personalization that can tailor experiences and products to users in ways that more closely align with their preferences. Search will be less laborious. We’re effectively tending towards immediacy in the transactional cycle.

The transactional workflow is a fascinating example of how trade can be automated based on AI’s capacity for pattern recognition. It may begins with an internal camera or external scanner, but later sensors that detect chemical makeups will be placed on refrigerators to detect the absence of certain foods, and your fridge will automatically be replenished, the shopping and delivery conducted by autonomous agents, a process already exemplified by Amazon’s Prime Air drones. The entire storefront model may be reconstructed to suit these autonomous processes, where a warehouse model would be more appropriate, with autonomous systems that gather a person’s order and deliver it directly. Evidently, intelligent design will transcend the digital experiences into all realms, from the digital to the physical and everything in between.

And unless AI replaces all jobs, workers will be matched to the most fitting role and company culture without having to undergo the arduous process of courting companies that we all know and love. The human’s role in all cases will be reduced to executive decision making and creativity.
Lastly, complex transactions such as complex purchases like car buying and air freight require a myriad of variables and nuances to complete the purchase.
NOTE: The Build Workflow has its own tag in KEEP

Last but not least, there is the creational workflow, a.k.a. the build workflow. Dear to artists, inventors, designers, and creators, the build workflow is at play any time a person is trying to create or make something. Did you make coffee today? Writing a song? Creating a product? The build workflow is at hand, and it’s bristling with the outcome structures that give premise to AI design. If you can identify a build workflow in your business or product, then you have found a priceless AI treasure trove.
The essence of a build workflow is that it can be described in outcomes.

To demonstrate, let’s make coffee together – a hazelnut latte for kicks. The macro-outcome is to make coffee. Branching out of that, a quantum universe of sub-outcomes emerges: I have to add water to the coffee machine, brew the coffee, add frothed heavy cream, add hazelnut syrup, etc. In order for the macro-outcome to be completed, the sub-outcomes must be completed. The sub-outcomes have their own micro-outcomes. To froth the cream, I have to wash the frother, open a new heavy cream carton, pour it, push the froth button, allow it to aerate the cream, and pour it into the coffee – and only then is the sub-outcome of “add frothed heavy cream” completed. Even these micro-outcomes have milli-outcomes. To wash the frother, I have to remove the lid, wash the frothing disk, scrub old cream residue with the brillo side of the sponge, scrape excess froth into the coffee with a spoon, etcetera. Now we are four levels deep into the outcome structure of this seemingly mundane task.

What we have just explored is the atomic universe of the build workflow for making coffee. Within, we have found a tree-like outcome structure, which is the basis of Intelligent Design. And here is a key heuristic of AI/UX, that Jakob Nielsen may also support: Humans, as the paragon of cognition, think in terms of outcomes. We’re goal-oriented creatures. And therefore, AI experiences, to truly be intuitive and groundbreaking, must adhere to this outcome structure.
Edit: at each waypoint in an outcome map, is an opportunity for AI workflow augmentation or circumvention.

In the coming chapter, we’ll learn about the fundamental paradigm shift from traditional UX to AI/UX in the outcome-oriented information architecture, i.e., outcome IA, and we’ll also need a map of the territory which we’ll find as we touch on outcome mapping exercises and practices.

What you have are technologies that simply generate everything, and are missing out on wielding the full power of the substance because they fundamentally lack design. These principles will allow you to shape technologies in a way that is more useful.

Edit: As a side note, and I’m borrowing from Brad Frost’s terms in Atomic Design – “builds” are atomic and nested. Like atoms within molecules, the micro-outcomes (atoms) build up to and are nested within the sub-outcomes (molecules), going all the way up the tree to complete the organism.

We have learn, trade, and create as the primary verbs of these different unique workflows. They’re the primary workflows within transference of value. In a build workflow, you’re transferring essentially these modular components to your build.

The key question to understand is “what is the user trying to achieve?” What are all of the outcomes they could be trying to achieve? And think back to the outcome structure when we made coffee. Understand that each sub outcome is an opportunity for AI augmentation or circumvention.
Then AI can know each user, from what they’ve already done, do what they frequently like to do. Is there a structure that makes up their individual style? The AI can understand that.

In transactions, the potentially for a thing to occur relies on an even exchange of value. In the creational workflow, the potential has a lesser barrier for access – it’s simply, is it possible, yes or no? Can it be done? Should it be done?
Edit: Outcome architectures are ripe soil for GenAI. Build is after all a synonym of generate. They are a goldmine for AI augmentations.

As the Lead Product Designer on the GenAI team at dscout, I identified two build workflows, the research plan creation and execution, and the synthesis and storytelling workflow where UXRs build insight reports.

The ironic thing about these three pillars of user experience is that they interleave through only minor abstractions. For instance, in an informational flow, is a user not building their experience? In a transactional workflow, is a user not building a shopping cart, or in gamification building their power? Is the build not relaying information to the builder? Things that are purchased often contribute to a build. And transactions rely on information. You could also have a build that relies on transactions. So there is an interweaving, a complex interplay of information, transaction, and creation all melded together to form an intricate web of value exchange.Essentially there is an economy of data, some abstract pieces, and some tangible. And in AI design, there needs to be fluidity of this data, interoperability of disparate applications, non-monolithic data structures – in short, mirroring the human mind, which is still unrivaled in its intellectual power.
Goal is to briefly demonstrate the build workflow, and other workflows at play.

The Economy of Creation is the broadest level theme of this book, as it covers not only existing tech companies, but all people as creators in the future. I think this is better to explain creative principles holistically, rather than. It’s not just AI+UX, but also any product or content experience, encompassing musicians and other content creators, but also product creators, not limited to modern day tech monoliths.

There might be a handful of key outcomes that your users need to accomplish. On the Airblox RFP tool, freight forwarders needed the ability to create packages, complex orders optimized for revenue management across multiple carriers and shipping lanes. Underneath the prompt bar I put a little chip that says create package. Suddenly the UI changes above, instead of itemizing the conversation linearly, I simply begin the “create package” outcome flow. Here UI meets intelligence in a pre-configured generative UI as the shipping lanes that comprise the package appear, with filters allowing them to sort, make selections. If create package were a prompt, the obvious question an AI agent would ask would be, what lanes should go in this package. The UI infers this natural follow up question with a command-based interface that looks like this.

Essentially the GUI is a prompt facilitation tool. The lane selections are paired with other user preferences, such as fewest carriers, as clickable chips to configure the prompt input when the user ultimately clicks the “Generate package” CTA. Lanes + preferences. You can imagine the prompt syntax saying “generate a package with these 15 lanes, and give me the fewest carriers”. The LLM compares the lanes to 3rd party benchmark data, a historical performance data API for each carrier on each lanes, and voila! At any point the user can give verbal feedback in the prompt bar, as if they were a prompt engineer, “These are good, but give me even fewer carriers, and give 1/3 of the business to United Airlines”. Boom, the Airblox AI agent Ask Fred (you need a funny name) pops out 5 more package options with unique characteristics and data-viz analytics. The user is able to make the executive decision, but the AI agent does all of the heavy lifting when it comes to optimization.
This is an example of one core user outcome. There are a few more that Airblox facilitates and they all have unique approaches.

Prompt writing is the prodigal older sibling of this shadow input-modality that no one is talking about. It gets all the attention, the rewards, and the shadow gets all the hand-me-downs. I am referring to a second input modality, the inferential input modality. Nielsen called this command and non-command based inputs. Non-command based inputs are inferred through user behavior and gestures, as users flow within a semantic context. At their core, all inferential inputs are inherently symbolic, and that is key, because the symbol of a gesture, a movement, whether intentional or not is the trigger for the input. If someone swallows and winces, we might infer that they need a throat lasange or some tea with honey.

The evolution of GUI features extrapolates the components we have currently into their ultimate form, so to speak. It presumes that present day capabilities, of let’s say an autocomplete dropdown, breadcrumbs, links, or autocomplete typing, are in an embryonic form, that bears a functional resemblance to a common design principle at their heart. Semantic progressive disclosure. As an app observes the users intentions via their behavior, the AI can draw inferences and project future semantic paths to the user. It takes the kairotic point in the users topical space and time, i.e. where the user is semantically at present, and it projects outward from that possible futures, branching out from it’s contextual awareness of the user at the center.
If I am writing a song, and as a composer, I am holding the first chord of my song, let’s say some type of mystical Lord of the Rings elf chord, then the AI agent would infer from
This is the second input modality, which I call the inferential input modality.

And the key understanding what a user might want to achieve where, can be turned in the exercise of Semantic Data Mapping in a business’s topical domain. Understanding what all of the semantic possibilities are for users to want to accomplish in your app. Where does the mind go in your topic? Where can it go? If they are on topic A, which topics are semantic neighbors. Of course, as a large language model, your AI agent will already know many of these connections, and have much of this neighborhood mapped out. But, the AI is like Voldemort and the Eye of Sauron in that currently, it only has a body if you make it one. And that body can be built in semi-monolithic UX structures, that I call Outcome Protocols. We are not quite there yet in terms of dynamic Generative UIs that utilize the component in your design system to make Fluid UIs on the spot. So what we must do in the interim, we must manually “map” the semantic space in terms of what users are trying to accomplish, and then harvest key outcomes in that space. From here, you would design an outcome protocol for each outcome, which are semi-monolithic component organisms that facilitate the completion of those key user outcomes. And we reviewed this in the Chapter X on Outcome Protocols.

You must also draw connections between nodes to identify semantic neighbors which can show up as suggestions in some form of progressive disclosure. This can be done, however you like. You can use note cards, you can user a digital whiteboard, but you have to explore the depths of your business’s topical semantic domain, and harvest the gems within.

And of course, generative UIs are a general technology that will likely be conquered by a single company and provided as an API-based capability of children applications, so I would warn against going up against juggernauts like Adobe and Figma at solving that problem. Call to mind the warnings in Chapter X on these juggernauts expanding their value reach acting as a technological whirlpool for smaller companies, so your idea needs to be future-proof.
