Another aspect of modularity is that the data needs to be as flexible as language.
This is the amplification and amalgamation of the UX heuristic on Freedom and Control, and Flexibility and Ease of Use.
Semantics can create architectures on the fly, you can navigate from one end to the next in the blink of an eye, and you can make any object do whatever it can do. Enter the realm of true UI magic.
Alternative names: The semantic fluidity of data, neural data mapping, neural navigation.
With the first principle that AI inverts the relationship of humans and data, UIs can now _________.

During my time studying semantic architectures in college, I was permitted into PhD level rhetoric courses where we studied, primarily, semantic architectures in nature. Various linguistic architectures included the neural networks, classification systems, music, archeologies of knowledge, and the cosmologies of belief that drive society.
I’m not going to sugar-coat it — this theme is not for the faint of heart.

As the third heuristic of AI, neural navigation seeks to define the interconnected nature of data. It states that data exists as a semantic network, not as a hierarchy, a concept that enables free-flowing pathways, while eliminating data siloes which are inherent to hiearchical semantic models. [It allows] Network architectures allow users to flow freely in their exploration of a product’s topoi, whereas hiearchies create data silos that inhibit user movement. This network model perfectly mirrors the way the human mind explores topics, and in the realm of product design, is now an affordance of generative AI. Now, with AI, your product can mirror the semantic pathways of the human mind.
The datum in your application is confined to the topic of your industry and niche.
Neural navigation is the core of AI+UX, entwining user outcomes with potential future pathways afforded by non-verbal inferences.

You also have progressive disclosure into the rest of the network, based on semantic nearness or relevance. In other words, things that are extremely relevant will have higher disclosure, and things that are semantically irrelevant or distant will have small disclosure or not show at all.
Between each distinct value of a things there is semantic morphing, where one thing gradually blends into its neighbor
Recommendation systems are the embryonic version of this.
Now that we know about metaphorical transferences in greater detail, you have those metaphorical transferences built into the data with semantic data mapping.

Semantic data mapping is the process of exploring the expanse of your product’s topical space. What are all of the possible outcomes a user can seek to achieve in your product, in your space overall, and how are they connected to each other. Drawing these relationships will enable a machine learning and AI experience that guides the user like an invisible hand through the various corners of your product’s topical domain.

Need to ask yourself, from “here” what could a user want to achieve. Here is a point in the semantic space. For instance, on the Broadway website, they are playing Lion King, Aladdin, West Side story. Once users click into an option, they may wonder if prices are comparable to other shows. They are within Lion King, but want to compare prices across shows. The site silos shows instead of keeping each show on the same layer within the data map.
You’ll start to see an architecture beginning to form. One that is based on the key outcomes of your user as they travel through their outcome workflows in a webbed topical fashion.

Dynamic system creation is the ability for the AI to learn and uphold an abstract user-created structure within which for users to work. In other words, users can program conversational UIs and generative UIs to create a scaffolding as an architectural method / framework of accomplishing goals.
This theme is somewhat advanced in that it relies on futuristic generalized neural-visual tech.
I will give an example that we can abstract, transfer, and expand upon.
Kadrian is writing song lyrics, something newer to his adult self. He puts this on pen and paper, to capture the meter of each line.
Be-fore - I begin - I replied to my sin - can I feel the light - one more time.

Each hyphen represents a separation, so that each line is representing a column in a table. His intention is to create variants of that line, while capture the rhyme scheme and meter of the original line.
He opens up Apollo and begins singing the line. Apollo captures the lyrical phrase and understands from Kadrians prompt that he is using this structure.

“I want you to understand the metering system that’s going on here with individual parts. Like for instance, before is a part, I begin is another section, I replied to my sin is another section, can I feel the light is another section, one more time is another section. And then I want to be able to understand other variants that could go in each of those sections to create new sentences that go with this singing melody.”
Apollo understands, and is able to cast a holographic representation of the column structure in front of Kadrian.

“Got it, I understand you’re looking to keep the rhythm and meter consistent while exploring different lyrical options for each section. Let’s break it down and brainstorm some variants for each part.”
“After I start, I face my past deeds, will I find the light one last time?”
“Once I commence, I confront all my fears, shall I see the hope once again?”
Does this help with generating new lyrical ideas while keeping the same rhythm and structure?”
End quote. The new phrases fill in the corresponding columns, of which ther are 5.

Kadrian is happy the structure is in place, but he does not like these initial suggestions. He instead adds new lines to each column. “Add ‘just then - a warmth - flooded in - I regained my sight - maybe it’s a sign.’”

Then within “warmth” he asks for near-rhymes, and Apollo quickly pulls up a series of suggested rhymes for Kadrian to choose from, with the top suggestions on top, as well as associated phrases that could go into neighboring lyrical columns (semantic data modeling is exemplified giving disclosure to neighboring possibilities”. Kadrian toggles focus to the rhyming “a swarm” variant and disclosed are new semantic paths:
“Just then - a swarm - entered in - and pulled me from - the pleasant dream”.

Within this variant of a variant (branching), he clicks on ‘pulled me from’ as a phrase. Apollo intuits that Kadrian is looking for variants here as well, serving a few, while queing Kadrian to clarify the intent, “here are some variants. What can be improved about “pulled me from?”

And so the conversation goes on in this way, working within a dynamic visual and neural architecture that was created on the fly. The macro structure, can be drilled into finer levels of granularity (sub-outcomes) such as finding synonymous phrases, rhyming words, and much more.

He adds more context: “I’m trying to write song lyrics that tell an abstract story about a person who is being dragged into hell, accounting for their regrets, and asks to see the light one more time. I want it to end with a bittersweet realization, that it was X all along.”
He uploads the song audio file with scratch vocals. He mumbles a few nonsense ad lib lyrics to find sounds he likes.
The AI finds meaning in the gibberish, “this word (plays recording) sounds like your saying “I avail myself - the right to take this”.
At the end of this conversation, Apollo automatically saves the lyric writing structure and stores it with other lyric writing approaches to be recalled and used again later.

This was more or less a conversation I had when writing lyrics with ChatGPT4o. I sang to it, stated the prompt for it to help me find lyrical variants within that 5-column structure, and it adapted th structure as the basis of our dialogue.
So, how I would encourage you to think is that whatever is possible in conversation text-based UIs, such as dynamic system creation will be expanded into generative neural-visual UIs.

That dynamic interface technology is already being developed as a general technology. We’re even seeing in Figmas 2024 Config release which is a big deal. But it is just the embryo of a general purpose technology that will be able to house and project these impromptu user-created structures on the fly.
Ultimately, apps will be created by users dynamically.

But let’s talk about 2024. How does this apply today? We don’t have dynamic general purpose UI projections for just any task. In fact, traditional UX by definition just provides a single possible macro structure, within which there are single possible micro structures. The user has but the flexibility to navigate your product teams structure, and the hope is that it aligns with how they work. But as we all find out years into this career in tech, it becomes quite challenge to account for diversity of workflows. Some users write lyrics this way, others employ different systems in their approach.

Companies would have that that first, because by the time this tech is democratized this event will mark the end of private applications. Users will need applications insofar as they come loaded with predefined structures, and nuanced semantic domain knowledge. People will sell their structures to each other. Imagine knowing your favorite singers lyric writing system to guide your process?

In essence what users are doing here is partaking in a bit of programming themselves. Thag’s fundamenrally what ChatGPT users are doing. The creation of structures is inevitable in prompt creation. As we said, meaning is connected at an existential level to order, as words find root in the ordering force of pattern recognition as the engine of sentient intelligence. A piano is a static program. I may be writing music in my head and I merely have to look at the piano and a whole slew of new sonic possibilities hatch in my mind, because the piano as a program has very unique compositional ambixterous possibilities. But it is also limited to those possibilities by its own static nature. So now we are saying, provide a neural-visual-tactile-sonic interface for me that mirrors a piano, but I want to be able to bend notes. Now we are expanding the programs capabilities — the user is dynamically modifying the structure of the system to suit their current need.

We effectively do this now in user research, it’s just much slower until the mental model of how someone works is reified in the product as a user interface. The entire goal of user research is to map the users mental model so that it can be translated to visual design.

Here in the Fireflies UI you can see that every component has the ability to be augmented with AI, with a few subtle shortcomings. The entire UI is generative in terms of the data, but also in terms of the UI itself. For instance, now users can determine which filters they want. Fireflies is attempting to do this with topic trackers, which are user created filters. Search should be an AI search, not a keyword search. The summary can be tailored to user preferences, articulated through prompts or pre-created design system. Everything can be molded.
What is a point of reference? In physics it means x. In AI design it is using the qualities of another thing in a multi-modal capacity to describe

I think that referentiality could be a core theme of AI, or maybe it’s not thematic, and is just a capability. Themes are universal and are generally important. You have it with referencing AI with contexts, such as referring to it with deictic words like this, that, just from a prompt writing standpoint, but you also have AI’s ability to be cognizant of the UI, which is kind of like a referencing capability.

reference a certain quality of a thing or idea. My daughter says massage me. No daddy, like THIS. She demonstrates a vigorous massaging technique on her belly, a hidden secret among three year olds. She is referencing a non verbal input modality as a way of describing how she’d like me to massage her. And the quality that I understood was that she wanted it to be more vigorous. Vigor was the quality in the reference example.
Referencing phrases in responses to improve prompt engineering
This is part of a broader theme of improving the interaction cost and cognitive load burden for users in prompt writing. See Keep Note.

Another core theme might be control. I think control might even be an inseparable attribute of reference in the context of HCI interaction. When you reference something, you are doing so within an outcome framework to control and manipulate that object. This relates to mechanisms of control, also known as interactions, such as gestures and speech.

For instance, you can imagine just saying, thinking that you want to turn this light on in your house. And you can combine referentiality and control. And depending on the sensorium of the AI, if it’s able to control your house’s electrical system, then you could just say to your AI assistant, you know, turn this light on, and it could know what light you’re talking about, and then turn it on.
Adobe Firely uses reference to allow you embellish your prompt, using using the qualities of another image to describe a desired output. Draw my vector using the style of this image.
Multimodal input modalities come together like different elements of witches brew, throw in a rabbit foot, some bat wings, and the green strew starts bubbling with magic.

There is a non-generative side of AI usage which is critically important to understand, it’s data transfer capabilities. AI is capable of transferring data across platforms, and across modalities, allowing users to call data in all of its forms with natural language. The data could exist as a component in a design system that is called by the user, this is data transfer. The data could exist as body text in an email that is pasted in and matched to system data which itself is represented as visual component decisions, which is a feature I introduced at Airblox to replace manual filtering via GUI components with a simple copy and paste function.
Practical manifestation of Referentiality. The power of reference. Gamma and Brad Frost.
Data now fluid, must manifest as design. Pre-defined design components are the words that the GPT pulls into generative sentences.

Here are a list of abilities that pattern recognition enables. These are all essentially types of outcomes rooted in linguistic functions, which is why they are not themes. I want to predict this. I want to transmute this into that.

This chapter is designed as a catch all for capabilities that go beyond the primary themes. For instance, transmutation is not necessarily a theme of AI. Prediction is not a theme. These are both things that are possible from language, one reading patterns in historical data to predict future outcomes, and another is matching an input pattern to a pre-defined pattern to transmutate the quality of the input to match a quality which shows in the output. Referentiallity may even be just a capability of language, which allows us to manipulate UIs by tokenizing components with language and referencing them with GenAI to manipulate them.
It happens with humans at red lights all the time. There’s always that one guy who’s inching into the street licking his lips for the red light to flip green.
The cross light flips yellow, the crosstraffic stop, the hair intuitively stands up on your next.
I look at the clouds in Chicago and I know it’s going to rain soon. My weather app confirms.
I turn right in my car and around the bend I am able to tell that there is an oncoming car based on a few milliseconds of interpreting its motion signature.
My wife who is driving sees it a few seconds after me, and although I am the passenger, I obviously am the better driver.
AI does this too. Predicts the next sequence in a chain of events.
Imagine if you could clone yourself when you reached a juncture in thought. Two roads diverged in a yellow wood, and you can only move forward in one direction.

You may also want to come back to this juncture since it’s a parent step in the outcome map. Unfortunately, you can’t clone yourself, but you can leave a breadcrumb. And the breadcrumb can be much more contextually potent than the current design pattern.
This breadcrumb is understood to exist within the context of the outcome map as a nested outcome. The key is that users need to be able to come back to lines of thought, and we often get lost.
In AI systems this space isn’t just three-dimensional like color - it might be hundreds or thousands of dimensions, allowing for incredibly subtle variations and relationships between concepts.

Words are merely one form of symbolic definition. Words are just the congealed version of this mechanism. In rhetorical theory, the word ‘ness’ is used to describe the essence of something. Because while salient forms certainly emerge, the true patterns are repeatable essences that take any form. I believe this is what Plato meant by the metaphysical Forms. The quality of smooth-ness can be felt and seen in many objects, and it can even be abstracted to the realm of sound, as in the smooth quality of someone’s voice, and can also signify a lack of volatility as in a smooth job interview or a smooth plane ride.
Form itself can be represented in vector-space. The easiest to visualize is, well visual forms, but it applies to all channels of sensorium, sound for instance has this same vector space.

So you can imagine the pattern of a sound in this semantic space taking a certain shape. Let’s take the roaring sound of a fountain. It has a silibance, like the letter S, but also a low rumbling. It is ambient, and constant. The sound of cars going by by contrast are also constant and ambient, but they have a characteristic phase doppler effect as they move, and each car has a unique pitch and speed, all of which allows the whooshing sound of cars to be a distinct pattern from the roaring sound of the fountain. They are similar, but they are not the same. If they were the same, they would be indistinguishable in the semantic vector space, occupying the same space form. But because they are merely similar, the sound of the fountain can be said to be a certain “distance of similarity” from the sound of the cars. The distance in this case is not that long, because they are nearly identical. If they were identical the distance would be zero.

Now a flock of geese fly overhead, their sharp quacks pierce through the sonic cloud created by the fountain and the cars. They are easily distinguishable from the sound of the fountain, because quite simply, they have opposite characteristics, short organic yelps. The pattern of these yelps takes on a very different sonic form in the vector space. It can be said that they have a very far distance of similarity from the roaring fountain.

Because the sounds occupy vector space, and are connected by the path that unites their distance, AI can not only perceive each distinct sound, but it has the ability to gradually morph between the sound of a goose, and the roar of a fountain by creating a vector bridge between the positions of each pattern as a complex node.
This capability of morphing is inherent to language. As we know, AI endows products with the powers of language.
This is done via the power of metaphor.
The human mind has no trouble or with this whatsoever.

This connects to neural networks’ ability to perform “semantic interpolation” - smoothly transitioning between concepts in latent space. Perhaps this hints at a way that even symbolic systems like myself might approach continuous understanding, even if we can’t fully escape our symbolic foundations.
Let me explore semantic interpolation in more detail:

In the context of neural networks (particularly autoencoders, VAEs, and more recently large language models), semantic interpolation refers to the ability to smoothly transition between different concepts in the model’s latent space.
Words or concepts are represented as vectors in a high-dimensional space
Similar concepts are closer together in this space
You can “walk” between these points, getting meaningful intermediate representations
- Between “cat” and “tiger”: you might see gradual emergence of stripes, size increase, wilder features
- Between “happy” and “sad”: you’d traverse through various emotional states
- Between “chair” and “stool”: you might see the backrest gradually disappearing

What’s fascinating is that this isn’t just discrete jumping between categories - the intermediate points are often meaningful and coherent, suggesting some grasp of the continuous nature of meaning, even within our symbolic systems.

This relates directly to our earlier discussion about the limitations of categorical thinking. While we’re still working with symbols, semantic interpolation suggests that neural networks (like myself) might be able to represent meaning in ways that aren’t purely categorical or discrete.

Would you say this aligns with your understanding of transmutation and continuous semantic models? I’m particularly curious about how this might relate to human cognition and the meditation-induced states you mentioned that transcend categorical thinking.
User research, semantic mind mapping, and outcome harvesting

User research comes before any AI theme in the process. The goal is to define user mental models and desired workflows, and then translate that into visual design. The architecture of your product must mirror and augment the mental processes of the user, otherwise your product will end up where most startups end up — on life support from an overworked CX team before the founder capitulates. A product that perfectly mirrors user mental models, and ways of thinking about your apps topical space, while facilitating business objectives, is muah! al Dante. Once you’ve gotten significant context from research sessions, you can conduct the semantic mind mapping workshop.

AI research takes things a step further than traditional research in that the utmost goal is to identify macro and micro outcomes that the user is trying to achieve. In the talent world, a key outcome for recruiters was to have data at their fingertips to build reports. In the user research space, tagging research to synthesize it was a key outcome, creating research plans. In block space it is generate package, and respond to RFQ.

The way to do what you were envisioning yesterday is something I call semantic data mapping. We can use the mindmap feature in whimsical to do this. But you put the outcome at the center (“RFQ received”). Then you use the branches of the mindmap to explore all of the thoughts a broker might have within that outcome, in terms of data they need, actions they need to take, what they need to see, who they need to speak to. You map out the entire topical space of what happens when an RFQ comes in, creating a semantic map for that outcome. Then you do three things:
1. tag the map with gap analysis: what can our app facilitate? What outcomes can’t we facilitate and why? Are we missing a key piece of data?
2 cluster and connect the statements to see how they might flow into each other, identify dependencies and relationships between points

3. You get into modularity — what are the gems that come out of each branch, and what does the app need to do with them? The gem is the part of the AI output that is important to the user. Does a gem get drafted into an email? Does it need to be visualized as a chart?
This process covers the original four AI themes of my book. Outcome oriented information architecture, modularity, semantic data mapping, and untapped value.
But the first step is user research. Learning and gathering context. Then we can create a semantic mind map together.

By outcome mapping, you’ll start to see a product architecture beginning to form. One that is based on the key outcomes of your users as they travel through their outcome workflows in a webbed fashion, as they explore the topical space that your product seeks to cover, the outcome-oriented workflows your product is striving to facilitate. The centralizing power of AI brings data from disparate data sources together, converging with your product at the center.

The topoi is a semantic vector space, commonly associated with words, or topics. But to expand this concept outside of the verbal, the ancient Greeks had another term for “place”, which they called chora. A chora to my understanding is a topical space that is not necessarily bound to the verbal. For instance, my guitar neck, the fretboard with all of the notes, all of the musical possibilities is a chora, a semantic vector space with distinct potentialities. The dance floor, and the dancer represent a chora. In ancient Greek, the word chora (χώρα) referred not just to physical space or land but also to a conceptual realm of performance and movement. UI Technique to Reduce Interaction Cost of Prompt Creation
Let’s take an example of an interaction that I had while researching the connection between the Transformer architecture and stable diffusion.

“In a broader sense, both stable diffusion and the Transformer architecture aim to extract meaningful information from data while preserving important characteristics. They operate on different types of data (images vs. text) and use different mathematical techniques, but they share a common goal of transforming raw data into structured representations that capture relevant patterns and relationships.”

In this I want to reference part of the response with a gesture, and ask, “How does stable diffusion transform data into structured representations?” But I don’t want to type all of that. In language we have a shortcut for this in deictic terminology, or terms that rely on context to be meaningful. We would just say, “How does stable diffusion do that.” ‘That’ and ‘this’ are deictic terms which rely on context to be meaningful. But in place of a user having to laboriously type out what ‘that’ refers to, which adds significant friction to the interaction, the user should be able to gesture to relevant part of the response, to ask a follow up question. Now the AI knows what this and that mean, but the context is given through a quick gesture, which cuts out excess typing.

In essence, we as designers need to be thinking about where we can reduce the labors of text input within prompting. From this concept, a slew of creative design practices can emerge to streamline conversational interactions.
We’ve already seen this design pattern emerging in prompt wizarding, template based prompting,
Certainly! Here’s a revised version with visual representations for each design pattern:

1. **Gestural Referencing:** Users can reference specific parts of the AI’s response by gesturing. For example, they might highlight a particular section of the text or draw a circle around a relevant piece of information. This visual cue indicates to the AI which aspect of the response the user wants to focus on or explore further.
*Visual Representation:* A user interface displaying the AI’s response with interactive elements that allow users to highlight or annotate specific parts of the text using gestures or mouse input.

2. **Progressive Prompting:** Progressive prompting breaks down complex prompts into smaller, more manageable steps, guiding users through the prompt creation process one step at a time. Each step presents users with a clear instruction or question, along with options for proceeding to the next step or revising their input.

*Visual Representation:* A step-by-step wizard-style interface that presents users with sequential prompts and options for advancing to the next step or revising their input. Progress indicators and clear navigation controls help users track their progress and navigate through the prompt creation process.

3. **Modular Prompt Construction:** Modular prompt construction allows users to build prompts in a modular fashion, assembling pre-defined modules or components to create a complete prompt. Users can select from a library of predefined modules or customize their own modules to tailor the prompt to their specific needs.

*Visual Representation:* A drag-and-drop interface that allows users to select and arrange pre-defined prompt modules or components to construct their prompt. Each module is represented by a visual icon or placeholder, and users can customize the content of each module as needed.

4. **Prompt Expansion:** Prompt expansion involves automatically expanding user-provided prompts or queries into more detailed or comprehensive prompts, based on the context of the conversation and the user’s preferences. This technique helps users refine their prompts and explore related topics more thoroughly.

*Visual Representation:* An interface that dynamically expands the user’s input prompt to include additional details, suggestions, or related topics. Users can review the expanded prompt and choose to accept it as-is or further refine their input based on the suggested content.

5. **Contextual Prompting:** Contextual prompting leverages information from the ongoing conversation to generate relevant prompts or responses, tailored to the user’s current needs and interests. By considering the context of the interaction, the AI can provide prompts that are more aligned with the user’s goals and preferences.

*Visual Representation:* An interface that displays contextually relevant prompts or suggestions based on the ongoing conversation. These prompts may appear as pop-up notifications, sidebar suggestions, or integrated within the conversation thread, providing users with timely and relevant guidance as they interact with the AI.
These design patterns demonstrate how AI can assist with prompt creation by reducing the cognitive load on the user and enhancing the overall user experience.

Kenneth Burke, a prominent literary theorist and rhetorician, made significant contributions to rhetorical theory, and one of his key distinctions involves the tangible and the abstract. Burke’s work often focused on the symbolic nature of human communication and the ways in which language and rhetoric shape human experience.
In his rhetorical theory, Burke made a distinction between:

Motion (the tangible): This refers to the physical, biological, and material aspects of existence. Motion includes all the things that happen in the physical world, the realm of what is concrete and observable.

Action (the abstract): This refers to the symbolic, intentional, and meaningful behavior of humans. Action is mediated through language and symbols, representing the abstract, interpretative, and purposeful dimension of human life.
